coredata.dc:title: A simple colorimetric and fluorescent “on-off-on” dual-mode sensor based on cyan fluorescent carbon dots/AuNPs for the detection of L-cysteine and Zinc thiazole
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Lili
Wei, Wanglong
Zhang, Hongmei
Xu, Jiyao
Zhao, Xihui
coredata.dc:description: Cyan fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs)were prepared from Glutathione (GSH) and m-phenylenediamine by one-step hydrothermal method. Interestingly, the synthesized G-CDs were modified with many functional groups, which can reduce Au3+ to Au-nanoparticles (AuNPs). The formed AuNPs led to fluorescence quenching of G-CDs because of internal filter effect (IFE). Furthermore, a fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode sensor based on G-CDs/AuNPs was constructed and further applied for the analysis of L-cysteine (L-cys) and Zinc thiazole (ZTH). The fluorescence of G-CDs/AuNPs recovered from “turn-off” to “turn-on”, which may be owing to the interaction of L-cys and ZTH with AuNPs, resulting in the aggregation of AuNPs and disappearance of IFE. At the same time, the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of AuNPs was reduced. The dual-mode sensing platform not only had high sensitivity and selectivity for L-cys and ZTH, but also conducted visual detection. The fluorescence and absorbance detection limits for L-cys and ZTH were 0.01 µM, 0.1 µM, and 0.035 mg/L, 0.05 mg/L, respectively. It should be noted that G-CDs/AuNPs sensor has been successfully applied to monitor L-cys and ZTH in skin lotion, milk, lettuce, tea and other real samples, proving the potential application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: G-CDs
IFE
L-cysteine
Zinc thiazole
Dual-mode sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of L-cys and Zinc thiazole
Possible reaction mechanism of G-CDs with Au3+
Sensing mechanism of G-CDs/AuNPs to L-cys

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HRTEM images and particle diameter distribution histogram of G-CDs. (B) AFM image of G-CDs and Height profile along the line in (B). (C-F) XPS full spectrum and high-resolution spectra of C1s, N1s, and O1s of the G-CDs, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of G-CDs with different concentration of Au3+ (0–200 μM). (B) Plot of values of F0/F1 at emission peak 485 nm versus the concentration of Au3+. (C) Linear plot of F0/F1 versus Au3+ in the range of 1–15 μM. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of G-CDs with different concentration of Au3+ (0–200 μM). (E) Plots of values of absorbance at 537 nm versus the concentration of Au3+. (F) Linear plot of Absorbance versus Au3+ in the range 1–100 μM.

(A) TEM, (B) HR-TEM, (C) SAED, (D) Particle diameter distribution histogram and (E) EDS spectrum of G-CD/AuNPs. (F) UV–vis absorption spectra, (G) Fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs and G-CDs/AuNPs, (H) UV–vis absorptionof AuNPs and FL spectra of G-CDs. Inset of (F): images of G-CDs (left) and G-CD/AuNPs solutions (right) under sunlight.

(A) FL intensity of G-CDs, G-CDs + L-cys, G-CDs/AuNPs and G-CDs/AuNPs + L-cys (30 µM L-cys, 100 µM Au3+). (B) FL emission spectra of G-CDs/AuNPs with various concentrations of L-cys (0–30 μM). (C) Plot of F2/F1 versus the concentration of L-cys. Insert: linear section of F2/F1 versus L-cys concentration (1–10 μM). (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of G-CDs, G-CDs + L-cys, G-CDs/AuNPs and G-CDs/AuNPs + L-cys. (E) UV–vis absorption spectra of G-CDs/AuNPs with different concentrations of L-cys. (F) Plots of maximum absorbance at 537 nm versus the concentration of L-cys. (Insert: linear plot of absorbance versus L-cys concentration (1–10 μM).
The preparation and application schematic diagram of G-CDs.
The calculated IFE parameters of Au3+ on G-CDs fluorescence.
Determination of L-cys and ZTH in samples.

head.ce:title: A simple colorimetric and fluorescent “on-off-on” dual-mode sensor based on cyan fluorescent carbon dots/AuNPs for the detection of L-cysteine and Zinc thiazole

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological Textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles
Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological Textiles

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological Textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: L. Sun and W. Wei contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cyan fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs)were prepared from Glutathione (GSH) and m-phenylenediamine by one-step hydrothermal method. Interestingly, the synthesized G-CDs were modified with many functional groups, which can reduce Au3+ to Au-nanoparticles (AuNPs). The formed AuNPs led to fluorescence quenching of G-CDs because of internal filter effect (IFE). Furthermore, a fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode sensor based on G-CDs/AuNPs was constructed and further applied for the analysis of L-cysteine (L-cys) and Zinc thiazole (ZTH). The fluorescence of G-CDs/AuNPs recovered from “turn-off” to “turn-on”, which may be owing to the interaction of L-cys and ZTH with AuNPs, resulting in the aggregation of AuNPs and disappearance of IFE. At the same time, the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of AuNPs was reduced. The dual-mode sensing platform not only had high sensitivity and selectivity for L-cys and ZTH, but also conducted visual detection. The fluorescence and absorbance detection limits for L-cys and ZTH were 0.01 µM, 0.1 µM, and 0.035 mg/L, 0.05 mg/L, respectively. It should be noted that G-CDs/AuNPs sensor has been successfully applied to monitor L-cys and ZTH in skin lotion, milk, lettuce, tea and other real samples, proving the potential application.

ce:section.ce:para: L-cysteine (L-cys)as an amino acid is necessary to maintain redox homeostasis in the body [1]. The imbalanced of L-cys can lead to serious human health problems, such as cystine disease, liver damage, growth and development problems, cardiovascular disease and so on [2–4]. On the other hand, Zinc thiazole (ZTH) as a new type of insecticide, is commonly used in cherries, rice, cabbage and other crops [5]. However, the excessive use of thiazolyl zinc will lead to the imbalance of the ecological environment, causing great harm to humans and animals. However, till now few literatures mentioned methods about the detection of ZTH. At present, various detection methods including high performance liquid chromatography [6], mass spectrometry [7] and electrochemical method [8] have been developed. Nonetheless, these methods are limited by the expensive instruments and complicated sample pretreatment or easy to be interfered. Therefore, low cost, simple, rapid, accurate and efficient detection of L-cys and ZTH is of great significance [9,10].

Nowadays, the sensing method based on fluorescent nanomaterials provides a meaningful way. As a new type fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely utilized in biomedical, optoelectronic, catalysis, sensors and other fields because of their simple synthesis, low cytotoxicity, high photobleaching stability and chemical inertia [11,12]. CDs as sensors have been explored to detect metal ions [13], antibiotics [14] and pesticide [15], etc. The surface functional groups of CDs facilitated the interaction with the testing sample, resulting influorescence (FL) enhancement or quenching of CDs [16]. However, the unidirectional“on-off”mode was disturbed easily leading to the low selectivity and sensitivity [17]. Therefore, a fluorescence recovery mode (“on-off-on”) was investigated to improve the detection performance of CDs. Yan et al. designed a CDs-alizarin red system (off) to detect Pb2+(on) [18]. Reena K. Sajwan et al. established a simple, sensitive and fast fluorescence detection system with CQDs-AuNPs (off) for the detection of aldicarb (on) [19]. Lv et al. designed a kind of CDs as a fluorescent sensor for the continuous detection of Fe3+(off) and ascorbic acid (on) [20]. Although these reports showed relatively satisfactory results, [21–23], they were limited to single mode fluorescence response. To the best of our knowledge, the dual-mode sensor based on fluorescent and colorimetric method not only possesses high selectivity and sensitivity, but also is suitable for visual detection, making the analysis simpler and more accurate. For example, Jia et al. reported a new type of orange-emitting N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for the detection of nitrite (NO2 –) in a dual mode of fluorescence and colorimetry. NO2 – can cause the fluorescence static quenching of N-CDs. At the same time, N-CDs showed a visible color change (red-purple)[24]. Inspired by this report, it is a feasible attempt to develop a fluorescence and colorimetry dual-mode sensor to detect L-cys and ZTH.

In this work, cyan fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) were successfully prepared with GSH and m-phenylenediamine. The performance of CDs can be improved by doping heteroatoms to adjust the core and surface microstructure [25]. GSH is a tripeptide, possessing heteroatoms functional groups, which can be doped into the structure of CDs or modified on the surface of CDs, and endow more reactive sites to improve the analytical detection performance. Interestingly, after adding Au3+ to the prepared G-CDs, the color of G-CDs was changed from light yellow to wine-red gradually and the fluorescence was quenched due to the formation of AuNPs. Then, an “on-off-on” switch sensor based on G-CDs/AuNPs was established to detect L-cys and ZTH using the fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode sensing (Scheme 1 ). Most sensors are single-mode detection and provide only one output signal, with lower sensitivity and accuracy. This dual-mode detection method is not only accurate, sensitive and selective, but also realizes the visual detection of L-cys and ZTH, and the fluorescence and color changes can be observed by the naked eyes. More importantly, this sensor system has been successfully detected L-cys and ZTH in the real samples, showing an accurate detection result, and a promising application prospect.
Reagents

Glutathione was purchased by Love Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (shanghai, China), M−phenylene diamine, Gold (III) chloride hydrate (HAuCl4) were bought by National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Other metal salts [AlCl3, MgCl2, FeCl3, CaCl2, ZnCl2, HgCl2, BaCl2, CuCl2, CdCl2, Co(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, and KI] were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Factory (Beijing, China). Organic small molecule [Sucrose (Suc), Glycine (Gly), Vitamin C (VC), Urea, Glucose (Glu), Glutathione (GSH), Glutamate (Glut), Melamine (MIEL), Citric acid (CA), Lysine (Lys) Oxalic acid (OA) and L-cysteine (L-cys)] were bought from Fuchen (Tianjin) Chemical Reagent Co, Ltd (Tianjin, China). Pesticides [Kasugamycin (KSM), Boscalid (Bos), Carbendazim (Carb), Procymidone (Proc), Cyfluthrin (Cyf), Flonicamid (ARIA), Cyromazine (Cyro), Zinc thiazole (ZTH), Lime Sulfur (LS), Phoxim (Pho), Isoprocorb (Isop), Enidylpyridine (Enid)] were brought by Institute of Plant Protection, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences. All chemical reagents are readily available and do not require further purification when used.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were carried out on a JEM-1200 EX (JEOL, Japan) transmission electron microscopy. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was presented with a D/MAX-RB X-ray diffractometer, measuring with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) in the 2θ range of 10° to 80°. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed on spectrometer (NICO-LET5700) by KBr tablet method with scanning range 500–4000 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained with a scanning probe microscope (Nanoscope V Multimode 8, Bruker Corporation, America) under ambient conditions. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out on an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ThermoFisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). Fluorescence (FL) measurements were measured on a Fluoromax-4 Spectrofluorometer (HORIBA, Kyoto, Japan). The UV–vis absorbance was measured by a TU-1901 (Persee, Beijing, China). Fluorescence decay curve was recorded by an Edinburgh FLS980 at excitation wavelength of 410 nm.

G-CDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. Typically, 0.1 g p-phenylenediamine and 0.5 g glutathione were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water and then transferred to 20 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Heated the mixture at 220 °C for 18 h in a muffle furnace. The crude product was centrifuged with 10000 rpm/min and filtered with 0.22 μM membrane, and then, dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 1000, American Thermo Fisher) in ultra-pure water for 18 h. Finally, G-CDs in light yellow color were lyophilized to obtain the dry G-CDs product for further characterization and utilization. To verify repeatability, different batches of carbon dots were prepared under the same conditions. To reduce error, the following experiments were performed using the same batch of G-CDs and under neutral conditions.

HAuCl4 (Au3+) was added to the G-CDs solution (1 mg/L), after a few minutes, the G-CDs solution changed from light yellow to wine-red, confirming the formation of AuNPs. The obtained G-CDs/AuNPs composite was utilized in the following detection. Fluorescence spectra and ultraviolet absorption spectra of G-CDs/AuNPs were measured by fluorescence spectrometer and UV-vis spectrophotometer to further establish fluorescence calibration and colorimetric calibration curve, respectively. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded under excitation at 410 nm. The selectivity of G-CDs to Au3+ was explored by adding fifteen metal ions (Ba2+, K+, Co2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ni2+and Au3+) respectively. The concentration of metal ions was 200 μM. The anti-interference ability of G-CDs was investigated by adding Au3+ and other metal ions together into G-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of L-cys and Zinc thiazole
ce:section.ce:para: Different volumes of L-cys (10 mM) or ZTH (100 mg/L) standard liquid were added to the G-CDs/AuNPs composite. The final volume was maintained at 3 mL. The fluorescence recovery intensity of G-CDs/AuNPs at 410 nm excitation wavelength and UV absorbance under 537 nm were recorded. Other small organic molecules standard liquid (10 mM) [Sucrose (Suc), Glycine (Gly), Vitamin C (VC), Urea, Glucose (Glu), Glutathione (GSH), Glutamate (Glut), Melamine (MIEL), Citric acid (CA), Lysine (Lys) Oxalic acid (OA)] and pesticides standard liquid (100 mg/L) [Kasugamycin (KSM), Boscalid (Bos), Carbendazim (Carb), Procymidone (Proc), Cyfluthrin (Cyf), Flonicamid (ARIA), Cyromazine (Cyro), Lime Sulfur (LS), Phoxim (Pho), Isoprocorb (Isop), Enidylpyridine (Enid)] were added to G-CDs/AuNPs to evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference of G-CDs/AuNPs sensor to L-cys or ZTH.

The tap water came from the laboratory. Skin lotion and milk were bought from supermarket. Plum, grape, lettuce and tea leaves were taken from the market and washed with deionized water to collect the washing liquid. Then, the feasibility of detecting L-cys and ZTH by fluorescence colorimetric dual mode was tested by standard addition method. The addition of the standard concentrations of L-cys and ZTH were 3, 6, 9 μM and 0.3, 0.6, 0.9 mg/L, respectively.

The morphology and particle size distribution of G-CDs were characterized by TEM and HR-TEM (Fig. 1 A). G-CDs were quasi spherical with nicemonodispersity and an average particle size of 1.95 nm. The HR-TEM image revealed a good resolution lattice fringe spacing of about 0.21 nm. This fringe spacing can be attributed to the d spacing of graphene (100) surface, which implies the existence of spherical graphite carbon core [26,27]. In addition, the atomic force microscope (AFM) image (Fig. 1B) showed that the size distribution of G-CDs is narrow, and the height range is about 0.5–5 nm, which is consistent with the TEM result.

Subsequently, FT-IR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were preformed to investigate the possible surface functional groups and the chemical composition of G-CDs. As shown in Fig.S1, the broad absorption band centered at 3431 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH. The triplet characteristic peaks at about 2979, 2930, and 2854 cm−1 are ascribed to the asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of CH. The absorption peaks at 1703, 1605, and 1420 cm−1 were pointed to the stretching vibrations of CO, CC and C-N [28–30]. These data proved the existence of –OH, –NH2, and CO groups, which facilitates to improve the water solubility of G-CDs [31–33]. Furthermore, the XPS full scan spectrum of G-CDs in Fig. 1C displayed three characteristic peaks at about 285 eV, 400 eV and 532 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s respectively, and the contents of the three elements were 68.52%, 6.42% and 24.93%, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 1D) depicted four peaks at 284, 284.9, 285.79 and 288.3 eV for CC, CC, CO/CN and OCO/N, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) was observed with two peaks at 399.16 and 401.11 eV, which were attributed to porphyrin C-N-C and amino N in the form of N(C)3 or H-N-(C)2, respectively. Two peaks at 531.31 eV and 532.17 eV in O1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) were assigned to –COOH/OCC and COH/COC, respectively. The results of XPS verified that N was successfully doped into the structure of G-CDs, and the surface of G-CDs contained hydroxyl, amino and carbonyl groups [11,34,35]. These analysis of XPS agree well with those of FT-IR. The XRD pattern of G-CDs (Fig.S2) showed an obvious diffraction peak at about 2θ = 20°, which could be ascribed to the (002) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon, indicating the poor crystallinity of the G-CDs due to the functional groups of G-CDs [36,37].

The optical properties of G-CDs were investigated by UV-vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra. An obvious absorption peak at 340 nm was viewed in the UV-vis absorption curve of G-CDs (Fig. S3A, black line), which may be due to π-π* transition of CC from the carbon core and n-π* electron transition of CO or CN from the molecular surface section [38]. The optimal excitation wavelength (λEx) and emission wavelength (λEm) of G-CDs were 410 nm and 485 nm (Fig. S3A, red and blue line), respectively. And G-CDs were yellow under sunlight and cyan fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. Moreover, the emission spectra of G-CDs performed a slight excitation-dependent property (Fig. S3B), which usually depends on the size distribution and surface emission sites of carbon dots [21]. Additionally, the chromaticity (CIE) diagram of G-CDs was provided in Fig. S3C, the optimal CIE coordinates could be obtained (x = 0.15, y = 0.28), further confirming the distinct cyan emission of the G-CDs.

Furthermore, the effects of different batches, long-term storage, UV irradiation time, salinity and pH on the stability of G-CDs were explored. The FL intensity of G-CDs were not changed much in different batches under the same synthetic conditions (Fig. S4A). From the same batch, G-CDsshowed great stability after a month of storage (Fig. S4B) at room temperature. After continuous irradiation with UV lamp for 2 h (Fig. S4C), the FL intensity of G-CDs presented unobvious change, which confirmed the good photobleaching resistance. As seen in Fig. S4D, when the NaCl concentration changed from 0.5 M to 2 M, G-CDs possessed fluorescence stability, indicating the high salt tolerance. In addition, pH had an obvious effect on FL intensity of G-CDs (Fig. S4E). Under acidic condition, the FL intensity of G-CDs was higher than that in the initial prepared G-CDs (pH = 6). However, there was a good linear relationship between pH and FL intensity ratio when pH was from 6.0 to 14.0 (R2 = 0.96), and the linear regression equation was y = −0.11x + 1.6 (Fig. S4E insect). The results indicated that G-CDs was very sensitive to pH (6–14) and can be used as an indicator of pH. This may be due to the protonation or deprotonation of functional groups under acidic or alkaline conditions, inducing the destruction of electron transition of the luminescence center, consequently the fluorescence quenching. These excellent properties clearly demonstrate the great potential of G-CDs as a fluorescence probe in complex matrix analysis.

To explore the responsiveness of G-CDs to metal ions, the fluorescence and UV-vis absorbance spectra of G-CDs with Au3+ and other metal ions (including K+, Na+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Al3+) were measured. It can be seen in Fig.S5A, the effects of K+, Ni2+, Na+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Zn2+ and Al3+ on FL emission intensity of G-CDs were almost negligible. While, the fluorescence of G-CDs was quenched rapidly as Au3+ was added, and the FL intensity declined significantly, indicating a higher responsiveness to Au3+. What’s more, other metal ions have little disruption to this special responsiveness (Fig.S5B). Additionally, an obvious absorption peak appeared at 537 nm with the addition of Au3+ (Fig.S5C). Fig. S6 displayed the photographs of G-CDs with various metal ions under 365 nm UV light (top) and sunlight (down). Obviously, only Au3+ induced the fluorescence quenching of G-CDs, as well as the color of G-CDs changed from light yellow to wine-red, which may be due to the reaction of G-CDs with Au3+ leading to the formation of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs).

It was worth noting that the FL intensity of G-CDs decreased gradually as the Au3+ concentration ranging from 0 to 200 μM (Fig. 2 A). The relative FL intensity (F0/F1) at Au3+ concentration (0–200 μM) was presented in Fig. 2B, and exhibited an excellent linear correlation (R2 = 0.995) at the Au3+ concentration of 1–15 μM (Fig. 2C), where F0 and F1 were the FL intensity of G-CDs without and with Au3+. In addition, as shown in Fig. 2D& E, the absorbance at 537 nm increased with the increase of Au3+concentration from 0 to 200 µM, and described a good linear relationship between absorbance and Au3+concentrations (1–100 µM) with R2 = 0.991 (Fig. 2F). Moreover, the change of fluorescence and color of G-CDs with various concentrations of Au3+ could be seen with naked eyes (Fig. 2A and D inset), possibly due to the formation of more and more AuNPs. The above results demonstrated that G-CDs were suitable to be applied as a fluorescence and colorimetric probe for the quantitative determination of Au3+. The limit of detection (LOD) of Au3+ could calculate to be 0.029 µM and 0.01 µM by fluorescence and colorimetric methods, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible reaction mechanism of G-CDs with Au3+
ce:section.ce:para: The functional group (–OH, –NH2) on the surface of G-CDs possess certain reducibility, which can reduce Au3+ to Au, then forming AuNPs. The formed G-CDs/AuNPs complex were characterized. TEM image in Fig. 3 A displayed uniformly dispersed spherical AuNPs. Lattice fringes with interfacial spacing of 0.235 nm could be clearly observed in HR-TEM image (Fig. 3B), corresponding to Au (111) plane. Debye Scherer rings in selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern represented the crystal structure of AuNPs (Fig. 3C). Fig. 3D provided a histogram of the particle size distribution, showing an average particle size of 5.23 nm. Compared with pure G-CDs in Fig. 1A, G-CDs/AuNPs had larger particle size. And new characteristic peaks of Au appeared at 2.2 and 9.8 keV in EDS spectrum (Fig. 3E) [39]. It should be noted that the presence of Cu in EDS spectrum was due to the copper grid. Furthermore, the prominent absorption peak at 537 nm in the UV-vis absorption spectra was consistent with the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption band of AuNPs [40], and the color of solution changed from light yellow to wine-red (Fig. 3F). Although, the FT-IR spectra G-CDs/AuNPs showed no significant difference with G-CDs (Fig. S1), the XRD pattern of G-CDs/AuNPs displayed four diffraction peaks at 2θ = 38.4°, 45°, 65.2° and 78.6°, corresponding to (111), (200), (220) and (311) lattice planes of AuNPs [41], respectively (Fig. S2). All these results further confirming the formation of AuNPs.

According to the interaction between CDs and quenchers, the fluorescence quenching mechanisms of CDs can be divided into static quenching [42], dynamic quenching [43], photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [44], internal filter effect (IFE) [17] and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). In order to clarify the fluorescence quenching mechanism of G-CDs by the formed AuNPs, fluorescence decay lifetime was performed. The average fluorescence lifetimes of G-CDs and G-CDs/AuNPs were 6.36 ns and 6.18 ns, respectively (Fig. 3G), and the difference less than 0.2 ns could be negligible, indicating the static quenching process [45]. Noteworthily, the UV-vis absorption band of AuNPs were highly overlapped with the emission wavelength (400–600 nm) of G-CDs (Fig. 3H). Therefore, the IFE process should be considered. To better prove the IFE quenching mechanism, the IFE parameters were calculated with the Parker equation as follows [18,35]:
F
cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 d
A
ex
1 -
10
- d
A
ex
10
g
A
em
2.3 s
A
em
1 -
A
- s
A
em
E
obsd
= 1 -
F
obsd
F
obsd , 0
E
cor
= 1 -
F
cor
F
cor , 0

In the formula, Fobsd and Fcor are the maximum FL intensity before and after removing IFE. The absorbances at the maximum excitation wavelength (λ = 410 nm) and emission wavelength (λ = 485 nm) are expressed by Aex and Aem, respectively. S is the thickness of the excitation beam. G represents the path length between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of the test tube. D refers to the width of the test tube. Eobsd and Ecor are the fluorescence inhibition efficiency before and after IFE, respectively. After obtaining each value, the influence of IFE on the quenching effect was analyzed by the correction factor (CF = Fcor/Fosd) and the difference value (△E = Eobsd-Ecor). Table 1 listed all the parameters. It could be seen that the values of CF and △E increased gradually when the concentration of Au3+increased from 0 to 100 μM, demonstrating the improvement of IFE because of the formation of more and more AuNPs. Consequently, the potential fluorescence quenching mechanism of Au3+ to G-CDs could be attributed to the IFE of the formed AuNPs.
The possible sensing application of G-CDs/AuNPs system was further

surveyed as a label-free sensor for detecting small organic molecules. Fig. 4 A presented the FL spectra of G-CDs, G-CDs + L-cys, NG-CDs/AuNPs and G-CDs/AuNPs + L-cys under the same experimental conditions. After adding L-cys to G-CDs, there was no obvious change in the intensity and peak position of the FL spectra of G-CDs, indicating no interaction between G-CDs and L-cys. However, the quenched fluorescence of G-CDs/AuNPs system was restored by L-cys, which demonstrated the possible interaction between L-cys and AuNPs. Additionally, 9 kinds of organic small molecules (30 μM) were added into the G-CDs/AuNPs system, respectively, and the FL recovery properties were measured (Fig.S7A). Compared with other small organic molecules, L-cys induced a significant increase of FL intensity, indicating an accurate selectivity of G-CDs/AuNPs sensor to L-cys. More importantly, the recovery of G-CDs/AuNPs FL intensity by L-cys did not interfere by other coexisting small organic molecules (M) (Fig.S7B). Moreover, the sensitivity of G-CDs/AuNPs sensor system to L-cys was investigated via the FL emission spectra and UV-vis spectra by adding various concentrations of L-cys. With the addition of L-cys from 0.1 μM to 50 μM, the FL intensity of G-CDs/AuNPs enhanced gradually (Fig. 4B). The relationship curve of the FL intensity ratio (F2/F1) versus L-cys concentration (0–30 μM) was displayed in Fig. 4C, where F1 is the FL emission intensity of G-CDs/AuNPs, F2 is FL emission intensity of G-CDs/AuNPs recovered by L-cys. It must be noted that an excellent linear correlation existed between F2/F1 and L-cys concentration (1–10 μM) with R2 = 0.982 (Fig. 4C inset). The LOD of L-cys could be calculated to be 0.01 μM based on the 3σ/K formula. Furthermore, the UV-vis absorption spectra of G-CDs, G-CDs + L-cys, G-CDs/AuNPs and G-CDs/AuNPs + L-cys was exhibited in Fig. 4D. After the addition of L-cys, the absorption peak of G-CDs/AuNPs at 537 nm disappeared. Moreover, with the increase of L-cys concentration, the absorbance of G-CDs/AuNPs at 537 nm descended gradually (Fig. 4E&F), and depicted a good linear relationship (y = 0.012x + 0.79, R2 = 0.991) in the range from 0.1 to 10 μM of L-cys concentration. According to calculation, LOD was 0.1 μM. The performance comparison of L-cys detection was summarized in Table S1. The G-CDs/AuNPs sensor revealed a comparable or even better detection limit and sensing range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of G-CDs/AuNPs to L-cys
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence recovery mechanism of G-CDs/AuNPs after adding L-cys was investigated. With the addition of L-cys, the color of the G-CDs/AuNPs changed from wine red to light yellow, accompanied by the formation of precipitation. Compared with the TEM images of G-CDs/AuNPs in Fig. 3A, the morphology of AuNPs changed from dispersed small nanoparticles to large particles (Fig.S8A). The small nanoparticles dispersed around the aggregated AuNPs may be the G-CDs. which could be observed clearly in the HR-TEM image of Fig. S8B, and the 0.22 nm crystal plane spacing ascribed to the (100) lattice distance of graphite carbon (Fig. S8B inset). A new peak of S appeared in EDS diagram (Fig. S8C), which is attributed to the L-cys. SAED (Fig. S8D) displayed the crystal structure of AuNPs. These results suggested that L-cys induced the aggregation of AuNPs, leading to the disappearance of IFE of AuNPs to G-CDs, while the fluorescence of G-CDs was restored. The reason may be due to the higher binding affinity of L-cys with AuNPs than G-CDs, breaking the original equilibrium system and prompting the aggregation of AuNPs to large particles.

Interestingly, G-CDs/AuNPs exhibited the sensing capability to ZTH (Fig. S9). The fluorescence of G-CDs/AuNPs turned from “OFF” to “ON”, and the FL intensity enhanced with the addition of ZTH, while other pesticides had slightly effect on the FL intensity (Fig.S9A). Furthermore, ZTH could still induce the recovery of G-CDs/AuNPs FL intensity in the coexistence of other pesticides, indicating the high selectivity and anti-interference to ZTH (Fig. S9B). Additionally, the fluorescence of G-CDs/AuNPs recovered continuously with the addition of ZTH (0–0.7 mg/L) (Fig. S9C). And a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.994) was obtained between the FL intensity ratio F3/F1 and ZTH concentration (0.01–0.7 mg/L), where F1 and F3 were the FL intensity of G-CDs/AuNPs in the absence or presence of ZTH (Fig. S9D). According to the linear regression equation y = 0.30x + 1.01, the LOD can be calculated as 0.035 mg/L. Notably, the UV-vis absorbance of G-CDs/AuNPs declined gradually with the increase of ZTH concentration (0–3 mg/L) (Fig. S9E &F). It existed a linear relationship in the concentration range of 0.3–3 mg/L (y = −0.024x + 0.6, R2 = 0.993), and the LOD was 0.05 mg/L. Therefore, the results of fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode sensing proved the feasibility of G-CDs/AuNPs detection for ZTH, which could be further applied to the detection of ZTH in crops.

It was similar to the reaction phenomenon of L-cys and G-CDs/AuNPs that precipitation also appeared after the addition of ZTH to G-CDs/AuNPs. After standing, the precipitation gradually settled. And the upper liquid appeared pale yellow and fluorescence, resembling G-CDs. Significantly, the structural formula of ZTH also contained -SH, –NH2, indicating that ZTH also has complexation ability. Therefore, it was speculated that the reaction mechanism is similar to L-cys. The addition of ZTH destroyed the equilibrium relationship of the G-CDs/AuNPs system, and complexed with AuNPs, leading to the release of G-CDs.

L-cysteine and Zinc thiazole are widely used in cosmetics and crops, respectively. Thus, it is of great significance to detect L-cysteine and zinc thiazole in real life samples and food. L-cys in tap water, skin care lotion, milk and grape samples and ZTH in tea, lettuce, plum and grape samples were detected by G-CDs/AuNPs probe. L-cys and ZTH were not initially detected in samples. Then standard concentrations of L-cys and ZTH were added to the samples and the regression equation was used to calculate the fluorescence, colorimetric standard recovery. The results were shown in Table 2 , with excellent recovery rate and low standard deviations. All these displayed that the fluorescent and colorimetric detection had high accuracy, which further verified the reliability and feasibility of the G-CDs/AuNPs fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode sensor to detect L-cys and ZTH in real samples.

In this work, G-CDs were prepared with GSH and m-phenylenediamine as raw materials. The synthesized G-CDs can interact with Au3+ to form G-CDs/AuNPs, thus constructing a fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode sensor to monitor and detect L-cysteine (L-cys) and Zinc thiazole (ZTH). The fluorescent calibration and colorimetric correction curves were obtained respectively according to the linear relationship between FL intensity, absorbance and L-Cys, ZTH concentrations, At the same time, the visual detection of L-cys and ZTH could be realized by the change of the fluorescence and solution color of G-CDs/AuNPs. More importantly, the dual-mode sensor provided good recovery and accuracy in the actual sample detection of L-cys and ZTH confirming the potential applicationof G-CDs/AuNPs sensor.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51503110) and State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University) (Nos. ZKT41 and ZFZ201815).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.107079.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
